coredata.dc:title: Tribological properties of graphene-modified with ionic liquids and carbon quantum dots/ bismaleimide composites
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Chao
Yin, Qing
Zhang, Wenbo
Bao, Yan
Li, Peipei
Hao, Lifen
Ma, Jianzhong
coredata.dc:description: In order to improve the tribological properties of graphene reinforced polymer composites, ionic liquids (IL) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were deposited on the reduced graphene oxide (rGO) nanosheets surface to ameliorate the compatibility between graphene and bismaleimide (BMI) matrix, as well as the stability of their self-lubricating transfer films. Their tribological properties were evaluated, and the wear mechanism was studied by means of surface/interface analysis, respectively. It could be found that CQDs/IL/rGO could improve the stability of the friction coefficient and decrease the volume wear rate of their BMI composites. When the filler amount was only 0.6 wt%, the friction coefficient and volume wear rate of CQDs/IL/rGO/BMI composites decreased by 41.2% and 94.7% compared with the neat BMI, respectively. Also, the CQDs/IL/rGO/BMI composites exhibited the lowest friction coefficient fluctuations and volume wear rate than those of other composites. Because of the synergistic effects of the CQDs, IL and rGO, uniform, continuous and steady self-lubricating transfer film could be formed during the friction process, thus significantly improving the anti-friction and wear-resistance properties of CQDs/IL/rGO/BMI composites. More importantly, the unique combinations of advantages of each functional component can also be applied in many other fields, such as catalysis, chemical sensors, etc.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene
Carbon quantum dots
Ionic liquids
Tribological properties
Composites

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Numbers 22008145 , 22005233 , 21908141 and 22078188 ); the New Style Think Tank of Shaanxi Universities (Research Center for Auxiliary Chemistry and New Materials Development, Shaanxi University of Science and Technology ) (Grant Number ACNM-202110 ); and the Basic Industrial Science and Technology Projects of Wenzhou (Grant Number G20190008).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of CQDs/IL/rGO formation and preparing process of CQDs/IL/rGO/BMI composites. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Schematic diagram of the ring type on disc tribological tester. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
The FT-IR spectra of GO, CQDs, CQDs/rGO, IL/rGO and CQDs/IL/rGO, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Raman spectra of GO, CQDs, CQDs/rGO, IL/rGO and CQDs/IL/rGO, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
TEM images of GO (a), CQDs (b), CQDs/rGO (c), IL/rGO (d), CQDs/IL/rGO (e), and the EDX mapping (f–j) of CQDs/IL/rGO. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The SEM images of fractured surfaces for BMI (a), 0.6 wt% rGO/BMI (b), 0.6 wt%CQDs/rGO/BMI (c), 0.6 wt%IL/rGO/BMI (d) and 0.6 wt%CQDs/IL/rGO/BMI (e) composites, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
The average frictional coefficient (a) and volume wear rate (b) of the BMI and its composites. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Effects of rotational speed and load on the friction coefficient (a and c) and volume wear rate (b and d) of 0.6 wt% CQDs/IL/rGO/BMI composites. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
SEM images of worn surface for neat BMI (a), 0.6 wt% rGO/BMI (b), 0.6 wt% CQDs/rGO/BMI (c), 0.6 wt% IL/rGO/BMI (d) and 0.6 wt% CQDs/IL/rGO/BMI (e) composites, respectively.

XPS wide spectrum (a) and the high resolution spectra (C 1s (b), B 1s (c), Fe2p (d)) of the counterpart steel ring surface-bonded transfer film correlative to 0.6 wt% CQDs/IL/rGO/BMI composites. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Schematic illustration of the tribological mechanism of RGO/BMI, CQDs/rGO/BMI, IL/rGO/BMI and CQDs/IL/rGO/BMI composites, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
The final friction temperatures of counterpart steel ring surface with CQDs/IL/rGO/BMI composites. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

TGA (a) and DTG (b) curves of neat BMI, 0.6 wt% rGO/BMI, 0.6 wt% CQDs/rGO/BMI, 0.6 wt% IL/rGO/BMI and 0.6 wt% CQDs/IL/rGO/BMI composites, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Hardness of the CQDs/IL/rGO/BMI composites with different filler content. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Statistical table of changes in surface elements concentration of counterpart steel ring friction pairs before and after friction treatment.
Summary of thermal properties of composite materials.

head.ce:title: Tribological properties of graphene-modified with ionic liquids and carbon quantum dots/ bismaleimide composites

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Advanced Materials and Nanotechnology, Xidian University, Xi’an, Shaanxi, 710071, P.R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In order to improve the tribological properties of graphene reinforced polymer composites, ionic liquids (IL) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were deposited on the reduced graphene oxide (rGO) nanosheets surface to ameliorate the compatibility between graphene and bismaleimide (BMI) matrix, as well as the stability of their self-lubricating transfer films. Their tribological properties were evaluated, and the wear mechanism was studied by means of surface/interface analysis, respectively. It could be found that CQDs/IL/rGO could improve the stability of the friction coefficient and decrease the volume wear rate of their BMI composites. When the filler amount was only 0.6 wt%, the friction coefficient and volume wear rate of CQDs/IL/rGO/BMI composites decreased by 41.2% and 94.7% compared with the neat BMI, respectively. Also, the CQDs/IL/rGO/BMI composites exhibited the lowest friction coefficient fluctuations and volume wear rate than those of other composites. Because of the synergistic effects of the CQDs, IL and rGO, uniform, continuous and steady self-lubricating transfer film could be formed during the friction process, thus significantly improving the anti-friction and wear-resistance properties of CQDs/IL/rGO/BMI composites. More importantly, the unique combinations of advantages of each functional component can also be applied in many other fields, such as catalysis, chemical sensors, etc.

ce:section.ce:para: Polymer composites have attracted increasing attention due to their simple moulding process, high specific strength, good chemical resistance and outstanding fatigue resistance. They were widely used in the aerospace, automotive industry, electrical & electronic and construction fields [1–3]. With the rapid development of mechanical systems, the preparation of polymer composites with excellent tribological properties has been valued by many researchers [4,5]. Extensive researches have been reported that the introduction of lubricating particles into the polymer matrix can form a self-lubricating transfer film on the surface of the metal friction pair during the friction process, thus effectively improving the tribological properties of the polymer composites [6–8].

As a typical self-lubricating nanofiller, graphene has aroused the excessive work of researchers due to it can effectively improve the tribological capabilities of polymer composites at very low filler loadings [9,10]. However, graphene nanosheets can easily re-stack and damage under harsh friction and wear conditions, thereby losing their lubricating properties. Meanwhile, the inert surface structure of graphene also results in poor compatibility with polymer matrix, which makes it difficult to fully exert its lubricating effect [11,12]. Moreover, the large size of graphene makes it difficult to enter into the frictional micro-zone on the friction pair, which makes it difficult to form a uniform and steady self-lubricating transfer film. Therefore, improving the dispersibility of graphene in the polymer matrix, avoiding structural damage, and effectively filling the frictional micro-zone is expected to dramatically improve the tribological properties of its composites.

When ionic liquids (IL) are often used as lubricants or lubrication additives, the anti-friction and wear-resistance performance of the composite system can be significantly improved. This occurs due to its unique ionic compound structure, which enables the formation of a strong electric double layer adsorption film on the friction pair surface and promotes a tribochemical reaction film. Meanwhile, a multitude of studies have been conducted to modify nanomaterials by IL and exhibited better tribological performance through reasonable structural design. Li et al. [13] found that a robust macroscopic liquid super-lubricated state (μ ≈ 0.005) could be achieved through the synergy between IL and graphene oxide (GO). The adsorption layer formed by GO nanosheets and the boundary layer formed by IL act as positive friction-reducing and anti-wear material during the wearing-in period and result in the super-lubricated state under extreme pressure. Wang et al. [14] fabricated IL modified reduced graphene oxide (TrGO-IL) and used it for improving tribological properties of fluoroether rubber. Research indicated that IL's presence not only improves the compatibility between graphene and rubber but also improves the crosslinking density of the rubber. More importantly, the tribological performance of composites has been significantly improved.

As a new type of carbon nanomaterials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted more and more researchers' attention and been applied in many fields due to their excellent optical properties, wide sources of raw materials and simple synthesis methods and so on [15,16]. From the perspective of lubrication mechanism and microstructure, CQDs have a very small particle size, controllable functionalized surface, and low interlayer shear strength, making them a promising lubricating additive [17]. Especially the extremely small particle size, which is of great importance to solve the problem that traditional nanomaterials are difficult to enter the micro-concave area of the friction interface and form a smooth self-lubricating transfer film. Cai et al. synthesised carbon hybrids containing CQDs and GO by using one-pot pyrolysis of citric acid (TDCA), and used as lubricants in polyethene glycol (PEG) synthetic lube base oil. Due to the synergistic effect of CQDs and GO, TDCA exhibited excellent anti-friction and wear-resistance properties under high loads [18]. Therefore, the introduction of CQDs/IL/graphene hybrid material into the polymer matrix as lubrication filler is expected to utilize their synergistic effect to form uniform, continuous and steady self-lubricating transfer film during the friction process, thus enhancing the tribological properties of the polymer composites. As far as we know, the use of CQDs/graphene or CQDs/IL/graphene to improve the tribological properties of polymer composites has rarely been reported.

Based on the above research, CQDs/IL/rGO ternary hybrid was prepared by a facile method. Meanwhile, the enhancement effects of it on the tribological properties of bismaleimide (BMI) composites were first studied. More importantly, the enhancement mechanism of CQDs/IL/rGO hybrid on the anti-friction and wear-resistance properties of BMI was proposed. The introduction of IL improved the dispersion of graphene in the BMI resin and the stability of the self-lubricating transfer film, thereby effectively improving the tribological properties and changing the wear mechanism of the BMI composites. Moreover, the CQDs on the graphene surface could play a “nanometer self-healing” role during the process of friction.

Graphene oxide (GO) was synthesised from natural graphite flakes via a modified Hummers' method [19]. Citric acid monohydrate (CA, 99.5%), ethylenediamine (EDA, 99.5%) and 2-Bromoethylamine hydrobromide were supplied by Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. 1-Methylimidazole (99%), hydrazine hydrate (50%) and sodium fluoroboratewere (NaBF4, 99%) were provided by TCI (Shanghai) Development Co., Ltd. n-Butanol (99.5%), acetonitrile anhydrous (99.9%) and alcohol (99.5%) were obtained from Tianjin Tianda Chemical Co. Ltd. The Diallyl Bisphenol A (DBA) and 4, 4′-Bismaleimidodiphenylmathane (BDM) were supplied from Wuhan Zhisheng Science and Technology Co., Ltd.

Reduced graphene oxide (rGO) was obtained according to our previous research. 1.0 g of GO was dispersed in 1 L DI water and continued ultrasound for 30 min. Next, added 10 mL of hydrazine hydrate to the system and stir at room temperature for 24 h. At last, the product was washed with DI water for several times. The rGO was obtained by freeze-drying for 12 h.

5.04 g of CA was added into 54 mL deionised water and stirred with ultrasound for 30 min to dissolve thoroughly. Next, 1.8 mL EDA was added into the above solution drop by drop with stirring. Then, the mixed solution was transferred to a para-polyphenylene liner vessel and reacted at 200 °C for 5 h. After the reaction, the product was diluted with 150 mL of water and then subjected to dialysis treatment. Finally, part of the solvent was removed by rotary evaporation and freeze-dried to obtain CQDs.

1.64 g of 1-Methylimidazole and 4.92 g of 2-Bromoethylamine hydrobromide were transferred to a three-necked flask and reacted for 8 h at 35 °C under N2 atmosphere. The mixture was purified by n-Butanol. After the n-Butanol was treated by rotary evaporation, the product (1-methyl-3-aminoethylimidazolium bromide) was dried under vacuum for 12 h. Then, the mixture of 1-methyl-3-aminoethylimidazolium bromide and NaBF4 in the ratio of 1:1 was added in acetonitrile solution and stirred magnetically at room temperature for 6 h. The final product IL was obtained by suction filtration.

A classic synthesis of CQDs/IL/rGO hybrid, 1 g rGO, 5 g CQDs and 1 g IL were dispersed into 100 mL of water. The mixture was sonicated and stirred to make it evenly dispersed and then stirred and reacted at 50 °C for 6 h. After the reaction was done, the step of filtered, washed, and dried in a vacuum drying oven at 80 °C for 12 h was applied to obtain black fluid-like CQDs/IL/rGO nanocomposite. Furthermore, the preparation of CQDs/rGO and IL/rGO was similar to the above steps, nevertheless without adding IL and CQDs, respectively (The digital photos of the products refer to Fig. S1). The schematic of CQDs/IL/rGO formation and preparing process of CQDs/IL/rGO/BMI composites is shown in Fig. 1 .

The BMI-based composite materials were prepared according to the methods reported in our previous research, including pure BMI, rGO/BMI, CQDs/BMI, CQDs/rGO/BMI and IL/rGO/BMI composites with different fillers content [7].

The chemical composition of the samples was determined on Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR, VECTOR-22, Bruker) spectra and X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS, AXIS SUPRA, Kratos). The microscopic morphology of samples was observed using Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FE-SEM, S-4800, Hitachi) and Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM, G2 TF20 S-TWIN, FEI). Also, the thermal properties of the composites were analysed through a thermogravimetric analyser (TGA, Q500, TA) in an N2 atmosphere at a heating rate of 10 °C/min. The particle size distribution of CQDs was conducted on nanoparticle size and Zeta potential analyser (DLS, ZS90, Malvern). Raman spectra were recorded on a Raman spectrometer (Raman, DXRxi, THEM). X-ray diffraction (XRD, D8 Advance, Bruker) diffractometer was applied to the analysis of phase composition. The hardness of the samples was characterised with an MH-5 Vector microhardness meter.

The tribological properties of samples were investigated on End-face Friction and Wear Tester (MMU-10G, load 200 N, velocity 200 rpm/min) using a ring type on disc under dry friction condition at room temperature. The friction pair is the #45 steel ring (inner diameter: 20 mm, outer diameter: 26 mm), before each test, the surface of samples and counterpart steel ring was sanded with 600 grit sandpaper. The specimens were cut into the disc with a diameter of 40 mm and a thickness of 4 mm. The schematic diagram of the ring type on disc tribological tester is shown in Fig. 2 .

The friction coefficient was automatically recorded by the friction sensor (recorded once per 10 s). The average friction coefficient was the average value of the last 1 h of each test to indicate its steady-state values. The volume wear rate of the samples was calculated by Eq. (1).
(1)
W r
=
Δ m
L × ρ ×
F N
where △m is for the mass loss (g), L represents the rotation distance of the counterpart steel ring (m), ρ is for the density of the specimen in the test temperatures (g/mm3), and F
N is the loaded load (N). The weight of the specimen before and after the friction test was weighed with a precision balance with an accuracy of ±0.1 mg. After friction treatment, the surface temperature of the counterpart steel ring was tested by a TMP-2 multichannel temperature tester.
B represents the friction distance of the friction ring.

The FT-IR spectra were used to study the chemical structures of GO, CQDs, CQDs/rGO, IL/rGO and CQDs/IL/rGO. As shown in Fig. 3 , GO exhibits five clear absorption peaks at 1136, 1364, 1617 and 3630 cm−1, which belong to C–O–C stretching vibrations, C–H bending vibration, CC stretching vibrations in the benzene ring skeleton and –OH stretching vibrations, respectively [20]. The spectrum of CQDs is similar to that of GO. However, a new absorption peak appears at 1635 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibration peak of the C–N groups [21]. As for the spectrum of CQDs/rGO, the intensity of epoxy groups reduced significantly, indicative of most oxygen-containing functional groups on the GO surface have been removed after the reduction treatment. Compared to that of GO, the appearance of absorption peaks at 1088 cm−1 (stretching vibration of BF4 −), 1183 cm−1 (in-plane asymmetric stretching vibration of imidazole), 1456 cm−1 (bending vibration of CN) and 1629 cm−1 (stretching vibration of C–N) confirm the presence of imidazolium ionic liquid [22,23]. After introducing the CQDs, the peaks intensity of CQDs/IL/rGO at 1629 cm−1 increases, indicating the CQDs introduced onto the rGO surface.

Raman spectroscopy plays an important role in confirming the presence of carbon-based materials and has also been a potent tool for exploring their behaviour of electrons and phonons [13]. Raman spectra of the samples show both D (represents a defect in the atomic lattice of C) and G (represents the in-plane stretching vibration of C atom sp2 hybridisation) bands around 1345 and 1592 cm−1, respectively (As shown in Fig. 4 ) [24]. Raman ID/IG ratios are widely used to judge the quality of the carbon materials. When the GO reduced to rGO, the intensity ratio (ID/IG) increases from 0.90 to 1.45. This can be ascribed to the removal of oxygen-containing functional groups on the GO surface during the reduction process, resulting in an increase in structural defects. After depositing CQDs on the surface of rGO surface, the value of ID/IG becomes larger. This occurs as a result of the surface of CQDs contains more defects structures than rGO [25]. However, after depositing IL on the surface of rGO, the value of ID/IG becomes smaller. This may be put down to the strong π-π interaction between IL and graphene makes it exist on the basal plane of graphene, which further affects the lattice structure of rGO [26].

As shown in Fig. 5 a, the GO nanosheets are highly transparent, indicating that the thickness of them is very small. Moreover, there are many wrinkles on its surface, with a typical peel-off tulle morphology. According to the HR-TEM results presented in Fig. 5b, the particle size of CQDs is relatively uniform, and the average size is about 2.8 nm. This is also confirmed by the results of the DLS analysis (Fig. S2). The image of CQDs/rGO shows that the CQDs are uniformly distributed on the surface of graphene. The interplanar spacing of CQDs in the HRTEM images of CQDs and CQDs/rGO is 0.241 nm, which corresponds to the (100) crystal of graphite carbon [27]. As for the image of IL/rGO, it serves to show the grey material is evenly deposited on the entire surface of the graphene nanosheets. This can be ascribed to the π-π stacking, cation-π and van der Waals interaction between the graphene and IL so that they can be firmly bonded together [28]. When the CQDs and IL are simultaneously deposited onto the graphene surface, the CQDs can not be seen clearly on the rGO surface. This is mainly due to the masking of IL. What is noteworthy is that both CQDs and IL are uniformly deposited on the surface of graphene nanosheets and are not distributed outside the nanosheets (Fig. 5c, d and 5e). This indicates that the interaction between graphene, ionic liquid and carbon quantum dots are strong. The EDX mapping (Fig. 5f-j) of CQDs/IL/rGO is further confirmed that the CQDs and the IL are homogeneously coated on the surface of graphene.

Solvent dispersibility experiments confirm that the modification can effectively improve the dispersion of rGO in organic solvents (Fig. S3). The good dispersibility of IL/rGO and CQDs/IL/rGO in organic solvents makes it easier to disperse in the BMI resin matrix when using solution blending technology to prepare their BMI composites [29]. For the sake of further observe the capability of dispersion of the filler in the BMI matrix, the fracture surfaces of neat BMI, 0.6 wt% rGO/BMI, 0.6 wt%CQDs/rGO/BMI, 0.6 wt%IL/rGO/BMI and 0.6 wt%CQDs/IL/rGO/BMI composites were studied by SEM, respectively. As depicted in Fig. 6 a, the fracture surface of neat BMI shows a river-like morphology, which is a typical brittle fracture feature. The addition of fillers in the BMI matrix results in great changes in the fracture surfaces morphology of BMI composites (As shown in Fig. 6). For the 0.6 wt% rGO/BMI (See Fig. 6b), there are many graphene aggregates appearing. This may be due to the poor compatibility between the rGO and BMI. As demonstrated in Fig. 6d, the fracture surface of 0.6 wt%CQDs/IL/rGO/BMI contains a large number of dimples, which is a typical tough fracture feature. Meanwhile no obvious aggregates are observed from the fracture surface, and the protruding graphene nanosheets are coated with a thick polymer. This means that CQDs/IL/rGO has good compatibility and interface interaction with the BMI matrix [30]. As the amount of fillers increases to 1.0 wt%, obvious larger aggregates appear in the rGO/BMI composite. While the fracture surface of 1.0 wt% CQDs/IL/rGO/BMI has no obvious aggregate (Fig. S4). The excellent dispersion of CQDs/IL/rGO and its great interface interaction with BMI enable it to effectively exert its strengthening and toughening effect.

Fig. 7 shows the tribological properties of rGO/BMI, CQDs/rGO/BMI and CQDs/IL/rGO/BMI composites. It can be found that only a small amount of fillers is added to the BMI matrix, the friction coefficient and volume wear rate of the composites will change significantly. When the proportion of fillers is 0.6 wt%, the average friction coefficient and the volume wear rate of the rGO/BMI are decreased to 0.25 and 15.2 × 10−6 mm3/(N•m), respectively. The average friction coefficient and the volume wear rate of CQDs/IL/rGO/BMI composites reach the minimum value of 0.20 and 2.51 × 10−6 mm3/(N•m) at 0.6 wt% CQDs/IL/rGO loading. It is worth noting that whether it is IL, CQDs or CQDs/IL modified rGO, they can effectively improve the friction reduction and wear resistance of their composites (See Fig. 7 and S5). Compared to neat BMI, the average friction coefficient (0.34) and the volume wear rate (47.3 × 10−6 mm3/(N•m)) of CQDs/IL/rGO/BMI decreased 41.2% and 94.7%, respectively. This phenomenon indicates that the lubricating filler can not only improve the wear-resistance of their polymer composites, but the lubricant fillers in the resin matrix can exert an excellent anti-friction effect [31,32]. Compared to those of CQDs/IL/rGO/BMI, the CQDs/rGO/BMI composite displays a lower average friction coefficient (0.17), but a higher volume wear rate (8.9 × 10−6 mm3/(N•m)) with the filling amount is 0.6 wt%. These can be ascribed to that the CQDs/rGO contains more “carbon” lubricated fillers than that of CQDs/IL/rGO at the same fillers content. Moreover, compared with similar composite materials, CQDs/IL/rGO/BMI composite can obtain a lower volume wear rate at lower filler addition (0.6 wt%). More importantly, the introduction of the CQDs/IL/rGO also improves the wear resistance of the resin matrix significantly better than most similar reports [33,34]. However, CQDs/IL/rGO hybrid nanocomposite has better interfacial interaction with BMI matrix than CQDs/rGO. With further increase in the amount of fillers, the friction coefficient and volume wear rate of the composites gradually increase. However, which are still below those of the matrix resin. These results may due to that a large number of fillers easily form aggregates in the BMI matrix and thus can not effectively exert their self-lubricating properties. Even more remarkably, the friction coefficients of CQDs/IL/rGO/BMI changing with rotational time are more stable than those of other composites (See Fig. S6). This is attributed to the excellent dispersibility of CQDs/IL/rGO in the BMI matrix, enabling it to form a uniform and stable self-lubricating transfer film during the friction process, thereby ensuring that the friction coefficient of its composites fluctuates less [35].

Fig. 8 indicates that the average friction coefficient of 0.6 wt % CQDS/IL/RGO/BMI as a function of rotation speed at a constant applied load of 200 N and as a function of applied load at a constant rotation speed of 200 rpm, respectively. As depicted in Fig. 8a and c, the average friction coefficients of 0.6 wt% CQDs/IL/rGO/BMI decrease with the increasing applied load and rotation speed. These may be mainly attributed to the higher load or speed conditions, which can promote the formation of more smooth friction interface and self-lubricating transfer film so that the friction coefficient of the composite material is reduced [36]. However, the volume wear rate of 0.6 wt% CQDs/IL/rGO/BMI composites shows a different trend compared with the friction coefficient (See Fig. 8b and d).

Regarding the influence of rotation speed, as the rotation speed increases, more friction leads to more wear, but the wear debris fails to form an effective self-lubricating transfer film, so its volume wear rate increases. Then, as the rotation speed is gradually increased, sufficient lubricating fillers can be precipitated during the friction process. An effective self-lubricating transfer film can be quickly formed and thus the volume wear rate of composites decreases. Nevertheless, more friction heat will be generated at the wear interface with the further increase in rotation speed, making the composite melt and make the self-lubricating transfer film easily pelt off, resulting in a higher volume wear rate. As for the impact of applied load, the rough counterpart steel ring will first cause great damage to the composite as the load increases. Then, as the applied load further increases, the wear surface is further compacted and becomes smoother, thereby reducing the volume wear rate of the composite material. Finally, with the applied load exceeds 200 N, the smooth wear surface will be destroyed, and the self-lubricating transfer film can not be existed stably, resulting in deterioration of its wear-resistance [37,38].

The wear mechanism of the polymer composites can be identified by microstructure variations in the worn surface. The worn surfaces of neat BMI, 0.6 wt% rGO/BMI, 0.6 wt% CQDs/rGO/BMI, 0.6 wt% IL/rGO/BMI and 0.6 wt% CQDs/IL/rGO/BMI composites under an applied load of 200 N and dry friction condition is demonstrated in Fig. 9 . As can be seen from Fig. 9a, the worn surface of neat BMI is rough and full of debris. This may be due to its low wear-resistance and heat-resistance, and severe plastic deformation of the worn surface. The tribological mechanism of it is mainly attributed to two kinds of adhesive wear and fatigue wear. Compared with neat BMI, the wear surface of 0.6 wt% rGO/BMI composite is significantly smoother (See Fig. 9b). Although there is no serious damage on the surface, it still contains larger debris and deep wear marks, which shows that the reinforcing effect and lubricating effect of rGO on the BMI are limited. This can be described as the poor compatibility of it between BMI matrix leading to it difficult to evenly disperse in the polymer matrix so that it difficult to form a continuous self-lubricating “carbon film” during the friction process. After the CQDs and IL are deposited on the surface of graphene nanosheets, the worn surfaces of their composites are significantly flattened (See Fig. 9c, d and e). Especially the 0.6 wt% CQDs/IL/rGO/BMI composite shows the smoothest wear surface, and there are almost no large wear debris or scratches. The tribological mechanism of it belongs to the abrasive wear mechanism. The preferable compatibility and interfacial interaction between CQDs/IL/rGO and BMI can not only exert its strengthening effect effectively and improve the load capacity of composite but also create a uniform, continuous and steady self-lubrication transfer film during the friction process. This is conducive to protect the wear surface of the CQDs/IL/rGO/BMI composite from ploughing and improve the wear-resistance, which has been confirmed by many researchers through a lot of research work [39,40].

As mentioned above, a good deal of frictional heat will be generated at the friction interface during the friction process, often causing tribo-chemical reactions, which will affect the tribological properties and mechanism of the material [41,42]. In order to investigate the chemical changes of the counterpart steel ring surface after friction with 0.6 wt% CQDs/IL/rGO/BMI, XPS analyses were used to study its self-lubricating transfer film. As shown in Fig. 10 b, the peaks appear at 284.5, 285.6 and 288.7 eV in C1s spectrum are assigned respectively to the C–C, C–N and CO groups deriving from either BMI matrix or CQDs/IL/rGO. The B1s XPS peak-fitting show four peaks at 187.9, 188.4, 193.7 and 194.9 eV, which are assigned to the FeB, Fe2B, B2O3 and BF4-, respectively (See Fig. 10c) [43,44]. The peaks at 712.7, 719.3, 724.5, and 710.8 eV in Fe2p spectrum are belong to the Fe3O4 and α-Fe2O3 (See Fig. 10d) [45]. B2O3, Fe3O4 and α-Fe2O3 are often used as a lubricant or reinforcing filler, so a more steady self-lubricating transfer film is formed during the friction process. The changes of element concentrations before and after friction on the surface of the counterpart steel ring are studied and summarised in Table 1 . N, B and F elements are deposited on the surface of the counterpart steel ring during the friction process. This may be the result of surface absorption of the IL. Where the disappearance of Si and Mn elements on the counterpart steel ring lubricated by the BMI containing CQDs/IL/rGO lubricating filler means that a continuous self-lubricating transfer film is formed on the surface of the counterpart steel ring. Correspondingly, the C element content is increased. This is mainly attributed to the CQDs and graphene form the transfer “carbon film” on the surface of the counterpart ring.

In order to explain the formation mechanism of the self-lubricating transfer film, a schematic model as shown in Fig. 11 is proposed. During the friction process, although the graphene nanosheets in the BMI composites can be precipitated and form a self-lubricating transfer film on the surface of counterpart steel ring, because of its poor dispersion in the BMI matrix and weak interfacial interaction with the counterpart steel ring (See Fig. 11a). Uniform self-lubricating transfer film will be formed and which is difficult to exist stably. The CQDs on the graphene surface can be peeled off the graphene surface and insert into the micro-concave area on the surface of the counterpart steel ring, thereby cooperating with graphene nanosheets to form a continuous self-lubricating transfer film (As shown in Fig. 11b). Low-energy electrons will escape from the contact points on the surface of the counterpart steel ring during the friction process, so its surface will be positively charged [46]. After the nanocomposites containing IL are precipitated out of their BMI composites, they will be firmly fixed at the position that has a positive charge on the surface of the counterpart steel ring. According to the principle of electrical neutrality, the nanocomposites containing IL will continue to gather on the surface of the counterpart steel ring, thereby promoting the formation of an electric double layer [30]. So, the presence of IL can not only enhance the interaction between self-lubricating “carbon film” and the counterpart steel ring but also can promote the rapid formation of the self-lubricating transfer film. It can be seen that CQDs/IL/rGO lubricating fillers can prominently improve the tribological performance of BMI, which can be ascribed to their synergistic lubrication, including film lubrication of graphene, interfacial bonding of IL and CQDs nano-filling [14].

As mentioned above, a great deal of heat generated during the friction process will destroy the stability of the self-lubricating transfer film. For this reason, the final friction temperatures of counterpart steel ring surface with CQDs/IL/rGO/BMI composites were investigated. As shown in Fig. 12 , the final friction temperatures of counterpart steel ring surface with CQDs/IL/rGO/BMI composites are above 100 °C. While counterpart steel ring surface with neat BMI composites has a lower final friction temperatures. This can be ascribed to the poor thermal conductivity of BMI matrix, and much frictional heat is absorbed into it, leading to severe adhesive wear. The final friction temperatures of counterpart steel ring surface with 0.6 wt% CQDs/IL/rGO/BMI composite is the lowest. This result is consistent with the best anti-wear property and the minimum friction coefficient volatility (See Fig. S5). The transfer film is rich in thermally conductive carbon material that not only conducts the frictional heat but also protects the substrate from thermal damage. The final friction temperatures of counterpart steel ring surface with CQDs/IL/rGO/BMI show irregular fluctuations with the increase of the amount of filler loading. This may be ascribed to that the friction final temperatures are not only related to the composition of the material but also to the magnitude and stability of the coefficient of friction.

Thermal stability is another important property that needs attention in the application of polymer materials in the field of tribology. It can be seen in Fig. 13 a that all the samples appear to have similar degradation profiles and no apparent weight loss below 350 °C. The summary of TGA results is listed in Table 2 . It can be found that the T5% and T10% of BMI composites are all lower than that of neat BMI. This is probably attributed to that the 2D graphene nanosheets can hinder the crosslinking density of the BMI resin during the curing process. Compared to neat BMI, the maximum degradation temperature (Td, max) of 0.6 wt% rGO/BMI is higher. This indicates that the thermal stability of BMI can be enhanced by incorporating graphene. However, the Td, max of 0.6 wt% CQDs/rGO/BMI, 0.6 wt% IL/rGO/BMI and 0.6 wt% CQDs/IL/rGO/BMI are all lower than that of neat BMI. These results may be ascribed to two aspects. On the one hand, the poor thermal stability of CQDs leads to the early decomposition of its BMI composites [47]. On the other hand, imidazole ionic liquid can catalyse BDM self-polymerization in the curing process of BMI, making it difficult for some DBA to take part in the curing reaction and reducing the cross-linking density of the system. Compared to neat BMI, the char yield of BMI composites is higher, which may be mainly due to the good carbon formation of graphene [48].

The hardness of the material has a certain influence on its anti-wear performance. Fig. 14 shows the effect of the loading concentration of CQDs/IL/rGO on the hardness of BMI composites. According to Fig. 14, as the amount of filler increases, the hardness of the CQDs/IL/rGO/BMI composites increases. This reason is that the good dispersion of the CQDs/IL/rGO in the BMI resin and the strong interfacial interaction with the polymer matrix, which can effectively play its reinforcing effect, thereby increasing the indentation resistance of the CQDs/IL/rGO/BMI composite [49]. The hardness of BMI composite with only 1.0 wt% CQDs/IL/rGO (51.7) increase by more than 49% compared with that of neat BMI (34.7). However, the wear-resistance of the composites is not consistent with its hardness, which is attributed to the fact that the tribological properties of the composites are not only associated with its wear-resistance, but also closely related to its self-lubricating properties.

In summary, we employed rGO to construct CQDs/IL/rGO hybrid, where the CQDs and IL were coated on carbon substrate. Compared with rGO, CQDs/IL/rGO exhibits better dispersion in BMI. Only 0.6 wt% of CQDs/IL/rGO nanoparticle was introduced into the BMI matrix, the average friction coefficient and wear rate of the composite are reduced by 41.2% and 94.7%, respectively. Based on the good synergistic effect between CQDs, IL and rGO, uniform, continuous and steady self-lubricating transfer film could be formed during the friction process. The results of friction temperatures, thermal property and hardness studies showed that the tribological properties of CQDs/IL/rGO/BMI composites are comprehensively affected by many factors. We believe that IL and quantum dot functionalized graphene may open a new route for fabricating polymer composites with superior anti-friction and wear-resistance to satisfy their dynamic friction device applications.

Chao Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Qing Yin: Writing – original draft. Wenbo Zhang: Investigation. Yan Bao: Investigation, Validation. Peipei Li: Data curation, Validation. Lifen Hao: Methodology, Software. Jianzhong Ma: Methodology, Supervision, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Numbers 22008145, 22005233, 21908141 and 22078188); the New Style Think Tank of Shaanxi Universities (Research Center for Auxiliary Chemistry and New Materials Development, Shaanxi University of Science and Technology) (Grant Number ACNM-202110); and the Basic Industrial Science and Technology Projects of Wenzhou (Grant Number G20190008).

J. Hazard Mater.

Chem. Soc. Rev.
